coredata.dc:description: The development of biomass material is an important approach to alleviating the excessive using of plastic packaging, by which the product could be more environmentally friendly and lower toxicity. In this study, we developed a biodegradable photodynamic antibacterial food packaging film using nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) synthesized from crayfish shells, combined with konjac glucomannan (KGM) and sodium alginate (SA). Casting method was used to prepare the composite film and results indicated that incorporation of N-CQDs significantly enhanced the mechanical and barrier properties of the film by reducing the number of micropores. The N-CQDs endowed the film with strong antioxidant activity and UV resistance. The DPPH scavenging rate of the composite film reached 77.92 %, while the transmittance of ultraviolet (300 nm) was reduced to 16.97 %. Furthermore, under blue light irradiation, the film exhibited excellent photodynamic antibacterial effects against Shewanella putrefaciens and Staphylococcus aureus, achieving inhibition rates of 99.2 % and 98.99 %, respectively. The film solution demonstrated no cytotoxicity, and the composite film preserved crayfish meat for up to 8 days at 4 °C. Furthermore, the film almost completely degrading in soil within 14 days. These findings suggest that the KGM/SA/N-CQD film is a promising degradable antimicrobial material for food packaging applications.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of biomass material is an important approach to alleviating the excessive using of plastic packaging, by which the product could be more environmentally friendly and lower toxicity. In this study, we developed a biodegradable photodynamic antibacterial food packaging film using nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) synthesized from crayfish shells, combined with konjac glucomannan (KGM) and sodium alginate (SA). Casting method was used to prepare the composite film and results indicated that incorporation of N-CQDs significantly enhanced the mechanical and barrier properties of the film by reducing the number of micropores. The N-CQDs endowed the film with strong antioxidant activity and UV resistance. The DPPH scavenging rate of the composite film reached 77.92 %, while the transmittance of ultraviolet (300 nm) was reduced to 16.97 %. Furthermore, under blue light irradiation, the film exhibited excellent photodynamic antibacterial effects against Shewanella putrefaciens and Staphylococcus aureus, achieving inhibition rates of 99.2 % and 98.99 %, respectively. The film solution demonstrated no cytotoxicity, and the composite film preserved crayfish meat for up to 8 days at 4 °C. Furthermore, the film almost completely degrading in soil within 14 days. These findings suggest that the KGM/SA/N-CQD film is a promising degradable antimicrobial material for food packaging applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: At present, the excessive use of traditional petroleum-based packaging materials has significantly contributed to environmental pollution, posing serious risks to both human health and the ecosystem [1]. Although effective food packaging can minimize spoilage-related food waste during storage [2], the reliance on non-degradable packaging exacerbates the issue of plastic pollution. This underscores the urgent need for developing green, biodegradable antimicrobial food packaging films. Such sustainable alternatives not only help preserve food quality but also minimize environmental impact. As a result, biodegradable with high antibacterial properties packaging materials have become key areas of research in food preservation [3,4]. Naturally occurring macromolecules like polysaccharides and proteins stand out as promising alternatives to conventional plastic-based food packaging films due to their biodegradability and edible properties.

Konjac glucomannan (KGM) is a widely studied substrate for packaging materials owing to its low cost, good biodegradable, and film-forming ability [5]. However, packaging films made from KGM have high hydrophilicity, poor mechanical properties, and weak antibacterial abilities, which limit its practical application [6]. To address these shortcomings, modifications using additives are often employed to form composite films. Sodium alginate (SA) is a promising additive for enhancing the mechanical performance of KGM-based films. The hydrogen bonding between the hydroxyl (–OH) groups of KGM and the carbonyl (–C=O) groups of SA enhances the mechanical properties and stability of the composite film [7]. For instance, Wang et al. developed a tea polyphenol/SA/KGM composite film that demonstrated improved mechanical performance, which was successfully applied for fresh-keeping of beef and apples [8]. However, despite the enhancement in mechanical properties, both KGM and SA lack inherent antibacterial activity. Enhancing the antimicrobial performance of KGM/SA composite films is a key research focus to make them more effective for food packaging applications.

Nowadays, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) prepared from waste biomass have attracted significant attention due to its ultralow cost and biocompatibility [9]. Nitrogen doping enhances the properties of traditional carbon quantum dots (CQDs) by passivating surface, altering energy gap and electron density, to improving its quantum yield (QY) and fluorescence lifetime [10]. Studies have shown that nitrogen doping introduces amide and amine groups to CQDs, enabling the disruption of bacterial membrane surfaces and thereby enhancing their antibacterial performance [11,12]. Zheng et al. reported that NCQDs exhibited significant antibacterial activity against Staphylococcus aureus and Bacillus subtilis, with minimum inhibitory concentrations (MIC) under light conditions of 20 mg/L and 100 mg/L, respectively [13]. Additionally, N-CQDs possess outstanding free-radical scavenging capabilities, which contribute to maintaining food sensory qualities such as flavor and color, making them highly suitable for food preservation applications [14]. These unique properties have prompted researchers to explore N-CQDs as active fillers in packaging films. Crayfish is a popular aquatic product in China. However, the shell accounts for approximately 40 wt% of the crayfish. In 2023, China generated approximately 1.2 million tons of waste crayfish shells, causing severe environmental pollution [15]. Our previous research demonstrated that crayfish shells, rich in carbon and nitrogen, are ideal for synthesizing N-CQDs [16,17]. Although CQDs have been incorporated into sustainable food packaging materials, the potential of N-CQDs for enhancing food preservation is still underexplored [18].

In this study, we synthesized N-CQDs from waste crayfish shells and combined them with degradable substrates (KGM, SA) to create a high-performance, biodegradable KGM/SA/N-CQD composite film. The N-CQDs were produced via a one-pot hydrothermal method, and their properties were thoroughly characterized. The N-CQDs were then incorporated as active fillers into the composite film, and the effects on the film's physicochemical properties and antibacterial activity under light and dark conditions were evaluated. We also assessed the biosafety, preservation efficacy for fresh crayfish meat, and degradability of the film. This research highlights the potential of using N-CQDs in sustainable food packaging and promotes the value-added utilization of waste crayfish shells.

Sodium alginate (Mw = 581,110 Da, Viscosity: ≥20 (10 g/L, 20 °C)/mPa.s, Chemically Pure) and konjac glucomannan (Mw = 9.67 × 105 g/mol; viscosity: 30,000 mPa·s at 1 % w/v) were acquired from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China) and Hubei Konson Konjac Technology Co., Ltd., respectively. The bacterial strains Staphylococcus aureus (CICC 21600) and Shewanella putrefaciens (CICC 22940) were purchased from the China Center of Industrial Culture Collection (https://www.china-cicc.org/). Living crayfish, averaging 35 ± 2.4 g in weight and 6 ± 0.5 cm in length, were purchased from Jingzhou Tianhe Aquatic Products Foodstuff Co.,Ltd. (Jingzhou, China). All experiments utilized ultrapure water, and other reagents employed were of analytical grade.

The N-CQDs were synthesized through the hydrothermal method as previously reported, with some modifications [19,20]. Waste crayfish shells were washed until colorless and odorless and then crushed into powder. The powder (5 g) was dispersed in 50 mL of ultrapure water, and the mixture were stirred for 1 h. The mixture was then allowed to react in a polytetrafluoroethylene-lined reactor at 200 °C for 12 h. Then, the mixture was centrifuged (Andreas Hettich GmbH & Co. KG, 78532 Tuttingen German) at 9500 ×g at room temperature (RT) with 10 min, and the supernatant was collected and filtrated via an inorganic membrane (0.22 μm). The solution was purified in dialysisbags (1000 Da, MGMD380010, Mesgen Biotechnology, Tianjin, China) for 48 h, and the dialysate was replaced every 12 h. Finally, N-CQD powder was obtained by freeze drying the resultant solution under dark conditions for 48 h.

To characterize the surface morphology and particle size distribution of N-CQDs, a droplet of N-CQD dispersion was placed on an ultrathin carbon film and observed using a field-emission transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL JEM-F200, Japan) at 200 kV. Particle size distribution and average size were then calculated using ImageJ software [21].

The concentration of N-CQDs was adjusted to 1 mg/mL using deionized water and added to a 2 mL quartz cuvette. Zeta potential was then measured using a zeta potential analyzer (Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90) under the following test conditions: Zeta Potential Detection mode, 5 times, Cycle 30, and interval time of 60 s.

The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption characteristics of N-CQDs were measured using a GENESYS 50 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The sample was placed in a 1 cm path length, double-sided transparent quartz cuvette, with a wavelength range of 200–900 nm and a resolution of 2 nm.

The photoluminescence properties and fluorescence intensity changes of N-CQDs were characterized using an F-4600 FL Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (Baens, Shanghai, China) equipped with a 150 W xenon lamp. The excitation and emission slits were set to 5.0 nm, with a scanning speed of 1200 nm/min. The excitation wavelength range was 360–460 nm, and the emission wavelength range was 300–700 nm.

The chemical structure of N-CQDs was characterized by Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR). 1 mg of the dried N-CQDs were thoroughly ground with 10 mg of anhydrous potassium bromide (KBr), and then pressed into a transparent pellet using a hydraulic press. Then a FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific Nicolet iS20, USA) were recorded in the range of 4000 to 400 cm−1 to analyze the functional group structures of N-CQDs.

The crystal structure was characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku SmartLab SE) with a scanning angle of 5–80° and a scanning speed of 1°/min. The elemental composition and molecular structure of N-CQDs were analyzed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha), with Al Kα radiation (hv = 1486.6 eV) as the excitation source. The XPS spectra were fitted using Avantage software (Version 6.8.0, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA), and elemental calibration was performed based on the C1s peak shift.

The yield of N-CQDs was calculated by dividing the mass of purified obtained after dialysis by the mass of dried crayfish shell powder (5 g) initially used for synthesis. The QY of N-CQDs was tested as previously reported [22], calculated using quinine sulfate (QY = 54 %, 0.1 M H2SO4 at 350 nm) as the standard sample.

KGM/SA solution was prepared by adding KGM and SA in different proportions (w/v) with deionized water and stirring continuously at 30 °C until they were fully dissolved. Subsequently, KGM/SA/N-CQD film-forming solutions were prepared by adding the N-CQD powder (w/v) to the KGM/SA solution. All film-forming solutions were subjected to sonication for 120 min at room temperature and then allowed to rest overnight. The films were then prepared by casting. The film-forming solution (15 mL) were poured into a 90 mm Petri dish (SANIFICO, USA) and baked at 50 °C for 6–7 h. Afterward, the dish was taken out of the oven and allowed to cool to room temperature. The films were then removed and stored at 25 °C with 50 % relative humidity until required.

To optimize the film-forming formulations of the composite films and improve their mechanical properties, an orthogonal three-factor four-level experiment L16 (34) was designed based on the analysis of the single-factor experimental data. The variables examined included the added amounts of KGM(A), SA(B), and N-CQD (C) complex with four different levels set for each factor. The tensile strength of the composite films was measured to identify the optimal component ratio. When analyzing the results, the tensile strength was used as the main optimization index to ensure that the composite film possessed a certain degree of toughness and high strength. Details of all factors and their levels in the conducted orthogonal experiments are listed in Table S1.

In a dry environment, the Attenuated Total Reflection (ATR) accessory was placed in the optical path of the FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS20, USA). The background air scan was first recorded, followed by the placement of the film sample surface in direct contact with the ATR crystal. The scan was conducted in the range of 4000–400 cm−1 to characterize the chemical structure of the composite films. The crystal structure of the composite film was characterized using XRD (Rigaku SmartLab SE). The scanning Angle is 5–80° and the scanning speed is 2°/min. The elemental and chemical composition of the composite films were characterized by XPS (Thermo Scientific K-Alpha, USA), the procedures and analysis methods were the same as described in Section 2.2.2.

Using gaussian 16 (https://gaussian.com/gaussian16/) software modeling to analysis the interaction between KGM and SA, and simulated calculation for their product. The functional used for optimization was M062X-D3, and the basis set was 6-311G*. The optimization calculations yielded a model with no imaginary frequencies, confirming that the model represents a local minimum energy point.

Surface and cross-sectional morphologies of film samples were determined by scanning electron microscope (LGJ-1ON-01, China). The different films were cut into 3 × 6 rectangles, which were subjected to a brittle fracture treatment using liquid nitrogen. Film samples with sizes of 0.5 × 0.5 cm were adhered to a copper stub with double-sided adhesive tape for testing. All samples were gold-sputtered before observations.

The tensile strength (TS) and stress–strain curves (Fig. S4) were determined with a universal material testing machine (INSTRON 5966, USA). The produced film samples were evaluated at 25 °C and 50 % relative humidity after being cut into standard rectangular strips of 50 × 10. The initial clamping distance was set to 20 mm, and the stretching rate was 50 mm/min. Five strips of each sample were tested, and the average value was calculated. The tensile strengths were recorded using the built-in software of the testing machine.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water contact angle measurements of different films
ce:section.ce:para: A contact angle meter (KRUSS DSA100, Germany) was used to measure the water contact angles of the composite films. A high-speed complementary metal–oxide–semiconductor (CMOS) camera (40 fps/s, Sony, Japan) was used for dual-focus continuous shooting. Light emitting diode (LED) cold light source with a wavelength range of 6000–6500 K was used for illumination, and room temperature was maintained for stable shooting. By employing a microsyringe, 1 μL of distilled water was precisely dripped onto the composite film surface. Each composite film's surface was examined at five separate places, and the results were averaged. The images of the distilled water droplets were recorded by the camera. The volume of the added distilled water was 1 μL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Oxygen and water vapor transmission rates of different films
ce:section.ce:para:
Water vapor transmission rates (WVTRs) were determined as follows [23]: each composite film was cut into 120-mm circular samples and sealed in a breathable cup filled with anhydrous calcium chloride. The cups were placed in a desiccator at 25 °C and 50 % relative humidity. Their weights were recorded every 12 h for 10 d until a constant weight was achieved. WVTR was calculated via the following formula:
WVTR =
∆ m · d
A · t · ∆ p
where Δm is the change in the cup mass (g), d is the average film thickness (mm), A is the area of the exposed film (m2), t is the time interval (12h), and Δp is the difference in the water vapor pressure between the two sides of the sample (kPa).

The oxygen transmission rate (OTR) was measured as follows [24]: an oxygen permeability meter (MOCON OX-TRAN 2/12, USA) was used to estimate the OTR of each composite film. The circular samples used for testing were cut to a length of 120 mm after drying. The experimental pressure difference was set to 105 Pa while the test temperature was 23 °C.

The absorbance of the composite films was measured using UV–vis spectroscopy (Thermo Scientific GENESYS 50, USA) at range of 200–800 nm. The light transmittance was calculated at 600 nm. The UV resistance of the film was evaluated at 300 nm. Three replicate measurements were performed for each sample.

The antioxidant activity of the film-forming solutions was evaluated by 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) assay, in which DPPH solution was mixed with the film solution and incubated for 30 min at RT. The absorbance at 517 nm was then measured by UV–vis spectroscopy. The DPPH radical scavenging rate was calculated using the following formula:
Scavenging rate
%
= 1 −
A 1
−
A 2
A 0
where A1 represents the absorbance of the sample after reacting with DPPH, A2 is the background absorbance of the sample solution, and A0 is the absorbance of the initial DPPH solution.

The growth inhibition rate was tested as previously reported with some modifications [25]. S. putrefaciens and S. aureus were inoculated onto tryptic soy agar (TSA) and activated twice using the streak plate method. Subsequently, single colonies were then isolated and cultured in tryptic soy broth (TSB) until reaching the logarithmic phase, after which the bacterial solution concentration was adjusted to approximately 109 CFU/mL. 410 nm (20 mW/cm2) blue light-emitting diode (LED) was selected as photodynamic antibacterial light source. The film solutions and bacterial solutions were mixed in a 20:1(v/v) ratio and then irradiated with the blue LED (light conditions) at a distance of 10 cm from the solution surface or wrapped in aluminum foil (dark conditions) for different lengths of time (5 min, 30 min, 1 h, 2 h, 4 h, 8 h). The solutions were then diluted to appropriate concentrations. Each solution (100 μL) was spread in a culture disk and incubated at 37 °C overnight, then the growth was monitored and recorded. Then the growth inhibition was measured via the following formula:
Growth inhibition
%
=
N C
− N
N C
where NC and N are the number of colonies in control group and experimental group, respectively.

A suspension of HepG2 cells in serum-containing Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) was inoculated in a 96-well plate, with pre-incubation for 12 h to allow the cell density to reach 90 %. After discarding the culture medium, the film-forming solution and N-CQDs solution was added to the well of the corresponding experimental group, followed by the addition of fresh culture medium. A blank and control groups were also established and cultured for 24 h. After removing the culture medium, 100 μL of fresh medium containing 90 % serum-supplemented DMEM and 10 % Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) reagent was added to each well. Following a 1-h incubation at 37 °C, the optical density at 450 nm (OD450) was measured with a microplate reader to determine the cell survival rate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preservation effects of different films on fresh crayfish meat

ce:section.ce:para: The crayfish were cleaned up in sterile water. The same size of crayfish were picked out, and the heads and intestinal veins were removed. After shelling the crayfish, the resulting crayfish tail meat was wrapped with different composite films at the top and bottom, and the ordinary PE film was used as the control group. The wrapped crayfish tail meat was quickly placed under the blue LED light source (410 nm, 20 mW/cm2) for 4 h and then stored at 4 °C in a refrigerator. Crayfish meat was photographed on days 0, 2, 4, and 8.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of total volatile basic nitrogen content and total bacterial colony count
ce:section.ce:para: The total volatile basic nitrogen (TVB-N) content in the crayfish tail meat was measured using the automatic Kjeldahl method, following the procedure outlined in the national standard GB 5009.228-2016. Briefly, 5 g crayfish tail meat was minced and mixed with 30 mL of distilled water. The resulting mixture was fully homogenized and centrifuged, and the produced supernatant was used to measure the TVB-N value. The obtained results were expressed in mg/100 g. TVB-N values below or equal to 20 mg/100 g were considered acceptable.

The sample preparation followed the same procedure as the TVB-N measurement but under sterile conditions with autoclaved materials. Bacterial suspensions were prepared by diluting the sample tenfold with phosphate-buffered saline (pH 7.0). A 0.1 mL aliquot of appropriately diluted bacterial solution was spread evenly on plate media in three different concentrations. The plates were incubated at 30 °C for 48 h. Total aerobic microorganisms were then quantified using the standard plate counting method and expressed as log CFU/g.

The pH of the crayfish tail meat was determined by a pH meter following the method outlined in the national standard GB 5009.237-2016. Briefly, 5 g of the crayfish tail meat homogenate was added in a centrifuge tube followed by the addition of an appropriate amount of distilled water. The supernatant fluid was taken for analysis after sufficient mixing and centrifugation.

The thiobarbituric acid reactive substances (TBARS) content in crayfish tail meat was assessed using a micro malondialdehyde (MDA) kit obtained from Nanjing Jiancheng Bioengineering Institute. An appropriate amount of the crayfish meat was accurately weighed followed by the addition of physiological saline at a ratio of 1:9 (m:v). The resulting mixture was thoroughly homogenized and centrifuged at 2000 ×g for 10 min (Andreas Hettich GmbH & Co. KG, 78532 Tuttingen German). The supernatant was collected for analysis, and the TBARS content of the samples was calculated.

The ordinary PE and different films were uniformly cut into 90-mm circular samples. They were then wrapped in 1 mm × 1 mm gauze and buried in soil with depth of 5 ± 0.5 cm. The temperature was kept at 25 ± 2 °C and relative humidity at 80 ± 2 %. Samples were taken out from the soil every two days for studying the degradation of the films.

All data were compiled using Excel 2016, while variance and significance analyses for the measurement results were conducted with IBM SPSS Statistics software (version 26, IBM, Armonk, NY, USA). A one-way analysis of variance was performed for the variance analysis, and the least significant difference method was utilized for multiple comparisons of significantly different indicators (p ≤ 0.05). The results were presented as mean ± standard deviation (SD).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and properties of crayfish shell-based N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Our TEM results showed that the N-CQDs had a particle size distribution of 2–9 nm, with an average particle size of 5.06 ± 0.96 nm (Fig. 1A). High-resolution TEM imaging showed lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.23 nm, which aligns with the (100) plane of graphite [26]. Moreover, the TEM images showed that the N-CQDs were spherical and well-dispersed without apparent aggregation, likely due to their negative zeta potential (−6.47 mV, Fig. 1B), which promotes electrostatic repulsion and prevents clumping. The XRD pattern (Fig. S1) showed a broad peak at 30° and a smaller peak at 38°, corresponding to the (002) and (100) planes of graphite, respectively [27].This indicates that the N-CQDs possess an amorphous structure featuring sp2-hybridized graphitic carbon cores, which contributes to their UV absorption capability.

The yield of N-CQDs from crayfish shells was 19.7 %, and the reported extraction yields from biomass typically range from 5 % to 30 %, depending on the material source and synthesis methods. For example, Zhu et al. achieved a yield of 16.5 % for lignin-derived carbon quantum dots synthesized using a two-step method. [28]. The quantum yield (QY) of N-CQDs synthesized from crayfish shells was 28.36 %, which higher than other biomass sources [26,29]. This enhancement is likely due to the specific reaction conditions and the protein-rich composition of crayfish shells, which offer stable nitrogen and carbon sources. Meanwhile, nitrogen doping can further improve the QY by modifying the band structure, increasing defect sites, and altering the electronic structure [30–32]. This implies that they can effectively generate photons upon absorbing light energy, which in turn triggers photochemical reactions to produce reactive oxygen species, thereby enhancing their antibacterial efficacy.

The FT-IR spectrum of N-CQDs (Fig. 1D) showed characteristic peaks at 3407 and 3205 cm−1 (OH and NH stretching), 1570 cm−1 (CO stretching), and 943, 895, and 835 cm−1 (CH out-of-plane bending), confirming typical CQD features. Peaks at 2143 cm−1 (CN or NN) and 1404 cm−1 (CN stretching) indicated successful nitrogen doping [33], while peaks at 626 and 769 cm−1 corresponded to oxygen-containing functional groups [34]. XPS analysis (Fig. 2 ) showed peaks for C1s, N1s, and O1s at 285.0 eV, 397.0 eV, and 529.0 eV, respectively, with sub-peaks indicating CC (284.80 eV), C–O/C=O (285.29 eV), CN (288.73 eV), CN (399.70 eV), NH (401.59 eV), CO (533.86 eV), and CO (532.37 eV) bonds. These results confirm that N-CQDs have a surface rich in hydrophilic groups, such as hydroxyl, amino, and carboxyl groups, consistent with FT-IR findings.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of the N-CQDs (Fig. S2) contained strong peaks at 240 and 332 nm, which attributed to π → π* transitions of the CC bonds and n → π* transitions of the CO bonds, respectively [35]. In addition, the N-CQDs exhibited a wide absorption range (380–800 nm), possibly because of their wide band structure and random energy levels [36]. The fluorescence spectra of N-CQDs (Fig. 1C) showed a maximum emission peak at 470 nm under 400 nm excitation. The peak remained stable in the 360–400 nm range but redshifted as the excitation wavelength increases from 410 to 460 nm, likely due to the narrow size distribution and surface passivation of the N-CQDs [37,38]. Compared to conventional CQDs which requires UV excitation, nitrogen doping can extend the absorption peak of CQDs from the UV region to the visible light region and expand the fluorescence excitation rang, making N-CQDs more suitable for food packaging applications [39,40]. Additionally, N-CQDs showed excellent photostability, maintaining fluorescence after two weeks of storage, and demonstrated long-term stability in aqueous solutions [32]. These properties make N-CQDs based on crayfish shells ideal for photodynamic sterilization films in food packaging.

Packaging films must have sufficient mechanical properties to ensure their integrity during food storage and transportation. We optimized the compositions of the composite film by taking the tensile strength (TS) as evaluation indicators and the KGM, SA, and N-CQDs concentrations as the influence factors. The results of the orthogonal analysis are listed in Table S2.

Based on the analysis of the indicators in Table S1, the order of impact on TS for each factor was B (SA) > A (KGM) > C (N-CQDs) from the values of R2. Furthermore, the optimal combination of k-values was B3A3C1. That is, the optimal KGM, SA, and N-CQDs concentrations to maximize the mechanical properties of the composite films were 0.7 % (w/v), 1.2 % (w/v), and 1.0 % (w/v), respectively.

The FT-IR spectra (Fig. 3A) showed that for the KGM film. The absorption peak at 1731 cm−1 corresponds to CO stretching in the acetyl group of KGM. In the KGM/SA film, the characteristic peaks at 1599 and 1404 cm−1 are attributed to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching of –COO in SA [8]. The peak at 2926 cm−1 corresponds to –CH vibrations, and the peak at 1026 cm−1 represents CO and C–O–C groups [41]. The significant variations in peak intensities between the KGM and KGM/SA films are due to interactions between KGM and SA, including hydrogen bonding between the –COOH groups of SA and the –OH groups of KGM, leading to an enhanced –OH peak at 3421 cm−1 [42]. Gaussian simulations also confirmed that hydrogen bonds are crucial in the film formation process (Fig. S3) [8]. Notably, with additional of N-CQDs, the surface functional groups of composite film did not change, indicating good compatibility between N-CQDs and the KGM/SA.

Fig. 3B showed the XRD patterns of the composite films. The XRD pattern of the KGM film contained a broad peak at 21.4° and a weak narrow peak at 31.6°. This indicates that the KGM film has an amorphous structure, which is consistent with the results of Wang et al. on the structure of KGM [43]. The XRD patterns of the other films also contained a broad peak near 21°, indicating that they also had an amorphous structure; however, the weak peak disappeared after introducing SA, suggesting a possible molecular interaction between KGM and SA [44]. Consist with FT-IR spectra, the addition of N-CQDs also did not alter the XRD pattern, indicating that N-CQDs are uniformly distributed within the polysaccharide network, likely through hydrogen bonding or physical filling [45].

The XPS results indicated that the C1s spectrum of KGM film (Fig. 4A) was decomposed into three peaks at 284.83, 286.29 and 287.80 eV, corresponding to CC, C–OH/C–O, and CO bonds, respectively. The peaks at 531.52 and 532.80 eV were ascribed to CO and C–OH bonds, respectively. In the KGM/SA film (Fig. 4B), an additional O–C=O peak appeared at 289.40 eV, indicating hydrogen bond formation between KGM and SA, consistent with FT-IR results. Due to the presence of SA, the Na Auger peak was observed in the O1s spectrum at 535.56 eV(B) and 535.60 eV(C). Meanwhile, the emergence of an N1s characteristic peak at 400.0 eV (Fig. 4C) followed the incorporation of N-CQDs, which further splits into two peaks at 399.9 eV and 400.9 eV, corresponding to CN and NH bonds, respectively. These findings suggested that N-CQDs were successfully intercalated into the polysaccharide network.

The microstructure of the films was analyzed by SEM. As shown in Fig. 3C, the surface of the KGM film was rough and uneven with large number of cracks. The cross-sectional SEM image also showed numerous thread-like cracks and pores. By contrast, the surface of the KGM/SA composite film was smooth and uniform, suggesting that the incorporation of SA enhanced the film-forming capability. The cross-sectional SEM image also showed fewer thread-like cracks and a more compact structure, further indicating that KGM and SA have good compatibility. The KGM/SA/N-CQD composite film also had a smooth and uniform surface, without pores or cracks, demonstrating that all constituents had good compatibility and were evenly distributed in the composite film.

The effects of interactions between the different constituents on the mechanical properties of the films were studied by comprehensive analysis of the TS. As shown in Table 1 and Fig. S4, the TS increased significantly after adding SA (10.17 ± 0.68 MPa → 38.98 ± 0.32 MPa). This was ascribed to the formation of strong hydrogen bonds (XPS results) between KGM and SA, which enhanced the intermolecular forces within the composite film, resulting in improved mechanical properties. Moreover, the TS of the composite film increased from 38.98 ± 0.32 MPa to 53.84 ± 0.23 MPa with the additional of N-CQDs. Khoshkalampour et al. produced a carbon quantum dot/gelatin film and the TS was 23.79 MPa which was lower than our films [46]. This may be attributed to their high specific surface area and internal disorder, which can enhance the crosslinking within the film, making it more compact [47]. However, our orthogonal analysis showed that an excessive N-CQDs content led to agglomeration, thus increasing the roughness of the film.

WVTR and OTR are key parameters for measuring the barrier properties of packaging materials [48,49]. The WVTR and OTR of the films are listed in Table 1. The better the barrier properties, the more the film can prevent oxygen and water vapor from entering the packaging, thereby reducing food degradation [50]. Owing to its hydrophilicity and porous microstructure, the pure KGM film had high water vapor permeability (7.23 ± 0.34 g mm m−2 h−1 Pa−1) and oxygen permeability (32.96 ± 0.86 m3 μm m−2 d−1 kPa−1). With additional of SA, the water vapor and oxygen permeabilities decreased to 6.84 ± 0.27 g mm m−2 h−1 Pa−1, 29.13 ± 0.92 m3 μm m−2 d−1 kPa−1, respectively. This may owe to strong hydrogen bonds. Moreover, N-CQDs significantly improved the barrier properties of the film, this is because the N-CQDs effectively filled the pores and cracks in the film, as indicated by the SEM results, and strengthened the crosslinks between the other constituents, resulting in a denser film [33,35]. Wu et al. developed a corn starch/carbon nanodots film with a 2 wt% CD concentration, which achieved similar antibacterial performance to our film, but their film exhibited weaker barrier properties compared to ours [51].

The water contact angle (WCA) is a key indicator of the hydrophilicity or hydrophobicity of a material [52]. As shown in Fig. 5A, the WCAs of the composite films were all <90°, indicating that they were hydrophilic. This was ascribed to the hydrophilicity of most of the film components. The WCA of the KGM film was 67.5°, because its molecular structure facilitated the diffusion and adsorption of water molecules, making its surface hydrophilic and wettable. The WCA of the KGM/SA composite film was 75.8°, which may be due to the crosslinking reaction between SA and KGM to form a stable composite structure. After adding N-CQDs, the WCA of the films decreased, which may be due to the high hydrophilicity of oxygen rich polar functional groups on the surface of N-CQDs (FT-IR results). Other studies also find this phenomenon, for example, Chen et al. found that the introduction of N-CQDs reduced the WCA of CS films from 81.3° to 77.3°. And the WCA of the films decreased proportionally with concentration of N-CQDs [25]. Ezati et al. prepared a CDs/chitosan/gelatin-based composite biodegradable film, which exhibited a water contact angle of 53.6°, indicating higher hydrophilicity than our film [53].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of UV resistance and antioxidant capacity
ce:section.ce:para: Ultraviolet (UV) radiation can trigger photochemical reactions in food, resulting in the degradation of color, texture, flavor, and nutritional quality [54]. Enhancing the UV barrier properties of food preservation materials is crucial for protecting food from photo-oxidation, thereby preserving its quality and extending its shelf life [55]. We investigated the UV resistance and visible light transparency of the composite films based on the absorbance of UV–vis irradiation at 200–800 nm [56]. The KGM and KGM/SA films had an unnoticeable absorbance in the UV region (200–400 nm). In contrast, the addition of N-CQDs significantly enhanced UV absorbance, indicating improved UV resistance (Fig. 5B). Afshar et al. developed a wheat starch/gelatin film incorporating 14 wt% graphene quantum dots (GQDs), resulting in a T300 of approximately 21 % [57]. The observed difference in UV shielding effectiveness may be attributed to the incorporation of nitrogen atoms into the carbon matrix, which modifies the electronic band structure by introducing localized states near the conduction band. This modification reduces the band gap of N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) compared to GQDs, enhancing their ability to absorb UV light. Additionally, KGM/SA/N-CQD film not only showed a higher UV shielding capacity but also maintained a visible light transmittance of 58.99 %. Compared to the currently used black anti UV cling film, KGM/SA/N-CQD film can ensure good visibility of food under light exposure.

Next, the antioxidant activity was measured based on the DPPH scavenging rate, as illustrated in Fig. 5C. The pure KGM solution had a DPPH scavenging rate of 41.36 % owing to its inherent antioxidant capability. The DPPH scavenging rate slightly improved after adding SA, reaching 48.64 %. Notably, the incorporation of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) led to a substantial enhancement in the scavenging rate, reaching 77.92 %. This significant increasing is attributed to the abundant amide and hydroxyl surface groups on the N-CQDs, which effectively participate in free radical scavenging. Specifically, hydroxyl groups can form stable DPPH–H complexes, facilitating the discoloration of the DPPH solution [58]. Compared to other films containing N-CQDs, such as chitosan/gelatin composites with carbon quantum dots, which reported a DPPH scavenging ability of 65.4 % at 1 wt% CQDs [53], the KGM/SA/N-CQD film demonstrates superior antioxidant performance. These findings suggest that the KGM/SA/N-CQD film is a promising material for mitigating oxidation-induced color changes and spoilage in food applications [59].

We evaluated the antimicrobial activity of the KGM/SA/ N-CQD composite film solution against S. putrefaciens and S. aureus by the plate coating method. Totally, the antibacterial effect showed significance after 4 h for our films. As shown in Fig. 6 , the inhibition rates of the solution without N-CQDs were only 8.59 % against S. putrefaciens and 7.21 % against S. aureus after 4 h. In contrast, the addition of N-CQDs significantly enhanced the bactericidal activity, yielding inhibition rates of 72.97 % against S. putrefaciens in dark conditions and 99.20 % under blue LED irradiation. Similarly, inhibition rates of 70.90 % for S. aureus in dark conditions and 98.99 % under blue light were achieved after the same duration. Han et al. developed a degradable film that required 20 h of exposure to demonstrate significant antibacterial activity [60], In contrast, our film exhibited over 90 % antibacterial efficacy within just 4 h of blue light activation, showcasing its rapid antibacterial response. Xiong et al. reported an antibacterial duration of 5 days for their film. However, KGM/SA/N-CQD film maintained antibacterial activity for up to 8 days, indicating a prolonged antimicrobial effect [61]. These results underscore the potent antimicrobial effects imparted by N-CQDs in the composite film.

The bactericidal effect under dark conditions was significantly weaker than that under blue light irradiation. This is because under dark conditions, the antimicrobial activity of N-CQDs primarily relies on their surface amide bonds and amino groups, which interact electrostatically with the phospholipids of bacterial cell membranes [33]. Conversely, under blue light irradiation, the N-CQDs not only provide surface functional groups for sterilization, but also generate additional reactive oxygen species (ROS). These ROS can damage cellular DNA/RNA by inducing oxidative stress or destruction of the outer membrane, leading to irreversible bacterial death [62,63]. Consequently, the composite film solution demonstrates a dual bactericidal effect under blue light irradiation, enhancing its sterilization capacity. Furthermore, the antibacterial effect was more pronounced against S. putrefaciens (Gram-negative bacteria, G−) than S. aureus (Gram-positive bacteria, G+). This difference can be attributed to the inherent antibacterial properties of KGM and SA, which are hydrophilic. The hydrophilic lipopolysaccharides present on the surface of Gram-negative bacteria facilitate stronger binding with KGM and SA, leading to bacterial rupture [64]. Overall, the results from the plate experiments indicated no colony growth in any group after 8 h, demonstrating the long-lasting and stable sterilization effect of the KGM/SA/ N-CQD film. While, many studies used active fillers to increase the antibacterial properties on films. The rosemarinic acid cross-linked gelatin film developed by Qiao et al. showed good antibacterial effects against Escherichia coli and S. aureus with an inhibition rate of 92.04 % and 93.79 %, respectively. However, its complicated manufacturing process limits its application in industry [65].

To evaluate the biosafety of the film, HepG2 cells were used as targets to determine the biotoxicity of the film-forming solutions by CCK-8 assay (Fig. 7 ). The survival rates of cells exposed to N-CQD solution (1 wt%), KGM, KGM/SA, and KGM/SA/N-CQD film-forming solutions were 92.75 %, 99.30 %, 98.04 %, and 95.21 %, respectively. This suggests that none of the film-forming solutions adversely affected the proliferation or growth of HepG2 cells. Although the cell survival rate decreased slightly after adding N-CQDs, its cytotoxicity was negligible [66]. This result demonstrates that N-CQDs are biocompatible, which can be attributed to the biomass source material and the absence of toxic chemical reagents in the synthesis process. Thus, it can be safely used in food-grade preservation films without toxicity to human health.

Fresh crayfish meat is characterized by its transparency, well-defined shape, and elasticity (Fig. 8A). However, with extended storage, the crayfish meat in the polyethylene (PE) group exhibited noticeable spoilage after 4 days, accompanied by a significant release of deteriorated liquid. By day 8, the PE group showed further degradation, with the meat becoming mushy and the appearance of black spots on the surface. The KGM and KGM/SA films demonstrated similar performance, with relatively poor barrier properties leading to substantial shrinkage of the crayfish meat after 4 days. In contrast, the incorporation of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) significantly enhanced the barrier properties of the film, effectively reducing water loss in the crayfish meat. As a result, the KGM/SA/N-CQD film exhibited minimal changes in meat quality over the 8-day storage period, attributed to its excellent antibacterial properties.

TVB-N (total volatile basic nitrogen) is a crucial indicator for assessing the freshness and quality of animal food products [67]. Elevated TVB-N levels typically indicate a decline in food quality and freshness, with a threshold of 20 mg/100 g considered unacceptable [68]. As shown in Fig. 8B, the TVB-N value in the polyethylene (PE) group starts at 3.85 ± 0.12 mg/g on day 0 and increases to 24.03 ± 1.59 mg/g by day 4, surpassing the acceptable range. By day 8, the TVB-N in the KGM/SA/N-CQD group reached 20.37 ± 1.14 mg/g, marking it as unacceptable, while the shelf life of crayfish in the KGM and KGM/SA groups was slightly higher than the PE group.

The total colony count data corroborated the TVB-N findings. A product is considered unacceptable when the total colony count exceeds 6 lg CFU/g. In the control group, the total colony count reached 6.68 ± 0.31 lg CFU/g on day 4, whereas the KGM/SA/N-CQD group exceeded the 6 lg CFU/g threshold on day 8, recording 6.14 ± 0.39 lg CFU/g (Fig. 8C). Guo et al. developed an elderberry anthocyanin film that extended the shelf life of fresh shrimp for no >5 d [69]. In comparison, the KGM/SA/N-CQD photodynamic preservation film extended the shelf life of crayfish up to 8 d. This demonstrates that, under blue LED irradiation at 4 °C, the KGM/SA/N-CQD photodynamic film exhibits superior preservation capabilities. The released reactive oxygen species (ROS) effectively and rapidly eliminate bacteria, reducing the initial total colony count in the crayfish, inhibiting TVB-N production.

As shown in Fig. 8D, the pH values of the crayfish meat in the PE, KGM, KGM/SA, and KGM/SA/N-CQD groups first decreased and then increased. At the initial stage, lactic acid, an acidic metabolite, was produced through microbial metabolism in the crayfish meat, decreasing the pH value [70]. Subsequently, the inherent proteolytic enzymes in the crayfish tail meat became active during storage (especially under anaerobic or microaerobic conditions), catalyzing the autolysis of the muscle tissues and producing alkaline substances (such as ammonia), which increased the pH value [71]. Under normal conditions, crayfish are considered unacceptable when the pH of the package exceeds 7.6 (Fig. 8D). During storage, the pH of the composite film group increased slowly as compared with that of the PE group, and the pH of the KGM/SA/N-CQD film group exceeded 7.6 on the eighth day. This may be due to the singlet oxygen released by N-CQD in the composite film, which effectively decreased the activity of the own enzymes and inhibited the autolysis process and production of alkaline compounds [72].

The quality of food, particularly fat-rich products, largely depends on the extent of lipid oxidation. Elevated lipid oxidation levels can result in undesirable odors, significantly impairing the sensory attributes and overall acceptability of such foods. Photodynamic antibacterial approaches often generate ROS, which may cause severe lipid peroxidation. For instance, when salmon was exposed to riboflavin concentrations of up to 50 μM under high irradiation rates of 1.27 kJ/cm2 and 2.76 kJ/cm2, significant increases in thiobarbituric acid reactive substances were observed, indicating severe lipid peroxidation [73,74]. TBARS analysis is an essential method for assessing lipid peroxidation in biological systems, which is particularly suitable for lipid-rich aquatic food [75]. According to Fig. 8E, the control and other composite film groups showed increasing trends as the storage time increased. The initial value of the TBARS content in each experimental group is 0.19 ± 0.02 mg/Kg, and the PE group exhibits a significantly faster-increasing trend than those of the composite film groups, reaching 1.43 ± 0.04 mg/Kg on day 8. In contrast, the TBARS of KGM/SA/N-CQD group only increased to 0.85 ± 0.03 mg/Kg at last. This outcome can be attributed to two factors: 1) The ROS released by N-CQD promoted the oxidation of enzymatic proteins in crayfish, thus affecting their catalytic activity and inhibiting the lipid peroxidation process. Additionally, ROS may interact with easily oxidizable amino acid residues in crayfish proteins, causing structural and functional damage to enzymes and inhibiting the lipid peroxidation process, which decreases the pH increase rate. 2) CQDs exhibit a unique dual mechanism of ROS generation and antioxidant activity [76]. While the ROS generated by N-CQDs may exert some oxidative effects on proteins, we carefully controlled the duration of blue light exposure during the preservation process (limited to 4 h). This ensures sufficient ROS production for antibacterial efficacy while allowing the antioxidant mechanism to mitigate excess ROS. This dual mechanism effectively balances antibacterial activity and minimizes potential oxidative damage to crayfish cells.

The degradation of the PE, KGM, KGM/SA and KGM/SA/N-CQD composite films in soil is illustrated in the Fig. 9 . The petroleum-based PE film remained undamaged and retained its original state during burial. While all the composite films surface became rough from day 6 to day 10, and visible cracks and holes were formed. After 14 d, all the films (b, c, d) were almost completely degraded. Cellulose-based packaging films are known to degrade within approximately 45 days [77], while hydrocolloid films often degrade more rapidly due to their distinct structural characteristics. For example, the film developed by Lai et al., based on κ-carrageenan and a curcumin-β-cyclodextrin complex, exhibited complete degradation about 8 days [78]. In our study, the developed composite film achieved biodegradation about 14 days. This relatively rapid degradation can be attributed to its lower crystallinity, as indicated by XRD analysis, and the lower molecular weights of KGM and SA. These structural features promote faster microbial or enzymatic degradation. These results demonstrate that the KGM/SA/N-CQD composite film can be effectively degraded within a relatively short period, which makes it a potential environmentally friendly food packaging material.

This study developed biodegradable film (KGM/SA/N-CQD film) by incorporating nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) synthesized from waste crayfish shells into KGM/SA. TEM imaging showed that the prepared N-CQDs were uniformly quasi-spherical with an average particle size of 5.06 ± 0.96 nm and N-CQDs possessed abundant functional groups such as hydroxyl and amide groups, with long-wavelength absorption peaks and good photostability. Optimal film formulations included 0.7 wt% KGM, 1.2 wt% alginate SA, and 1 wt% N-CQDs, achieving a tensile strength of 56.84 ± 0.23 MPa. The introduction of N-CQDs significantly improved the mechanical properties of the film and bestowed it with excellent UV resistance and antioxidant ability. In addition, owing to the photodynamic bactericidal activity of the N-CQDs, the composite film exhibited significant antimicrobial effects against S. putrefaciens (G−) and S. aureus (G+), with inhibition rates of over 98 %. Preservation tests showed that the KGM/SA/N-CQD film extended crayfish shelf life to 8 days while reducing pH and TVB-N levels. Additionally, this film demonstrated excellent biodegradability, with nearly complete degradation in 14 days. This work highlights a sustainable method for utilizing waste biomass in food packaging applications. However, excessive exposure to photodynamic effects may adversely affect meat tissue health. Future studies should investigate the underlying mechanisms in more detail and seek to identify an optimal balance between antibacterial efficacy and cellular protection. Moreover, given the potential edibility of the film, future research should assess its digestibility in the gastrointestinal tract and validate its safety through mouse feeding experiments.

Ziwei Jiang: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation. Jundong Feng: Validation, Methodology, Formal analysis. Yaodong Dai: Validation, Methodology. Wei Yu: Funding acquisition. Shunjie Bai: Investigation, Data curation. Chan Bai: Validation, Methodology. Ziyi Tu: Funding acquisition. Peng Guo: Funding acquisition. Tao Liao: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Liang Qiu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.
